question:
Show that for each positive integer  n,
\[
    n! = \prod_{i=1}^n  \mathrm{lcm}\{1, 2, \dots, \lfloor n/i\rfloor\} .
\]
(Here $\mathrm{lcm}$ denotes the least common multiple, and
$\lfloor x \rfloor$ denotes the greatest integer $\leq x$.)
solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
It is enough to show that for each prime $p$, the exponent of $p$ in
the prime factorization of both sides is the same.
On the left side, it is well-known that the exponent of $p$ in the
prime factorization of $n!$ is
\[
\sum_{i =1}^n \left\lfloor \frac{n}{p^i} \right\rfloor.
\]
(To see this, note that the $i$-th term counts the multiples of $p^i$ among
$1, \dots, n$, so that a number divisible exactly by $p^i$ gets counted
exactly $i$ times.)  This number can be reinterpreted as the cardinality
of the set $S$ of points in the plane with positive integer coordinates
lying on or under the curve $y = np^{-x}$: namely, each summand is the
number of points of $S$ with $x=i$.

On the right side, the exponent of $p$ in the prime factorization of $\lcm(1,
\dots, \lfloor n/i \rfloor)$ is $\lfloor \log_p \lfloor n/i \rfloor \rfloor
= \lfloor \log_p (n/i) \rfloor$.  However, this is precisely the number
of points of $S$ with $y=i$. Thus
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n
\lfloor \log_p \lfloor n/i \rfloor \rfloor
= \sum_{i =1}^n \left\lfloor \frac{n}{p^i} \right\rfloor,
\]
and the desired result follows.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We prove the result by induction on $n$, the case $n=1$ being obvious.
What we actually show is that going from $n-1$ to $n$ changes both
sides by the same multiplicative factor, that is,
\[
n = \prod_{i=1}^{n-1} \frac{\lcm\{1, 2, \dots, \lfloor n/i \rfloor\}}{\lcm
\{1, 2, \dots, \lfloor (n-1)/i \rfloor\}}.
\]
Note that the $i$-th term in the product is equal to 1 if $n/i$ is not
an integer, i.e., if $n/i$ is not a divisor of $n$.
It is also equal to 1 if $n/i$ is a divisor of $n$ but not a prime power,
since any composite number divides the lcm of all smaller numbers.
However, if $n/i$ is a power of $p$, then the $i$-th term is equal to $p$.

Since $n/i$ runs over all proper divisors of $n$, the product on the right
side includes one factor of the prime $p$
for each factor of $p$ in the prime factorization of $n$. Thus the whole
product is indeed equal to $n$, completing the induction.

descriptive_long.question:
Show that for each positive integer posintn,
\[
    \posintn! = \prod_{\counter=1}^{\posintn}  \mathrm{lcm}\{1, 2, \dots, \lfloor \posintn/\counter\rfloor\} .
\]
(Here $\mathrm{lcm}$ denotes the least common multiple, and
$\lfloor abscissa \rfloor$ denotes the greatest integer $\leq abscissa$.)
descriptive_long.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
It is enough to show that for each prime $primep$, the exponent of $primep$ in
the prime factorization of both sides is the same.
On the left side, it is well-known that the exponent of $primep$ in the
prime factorization of $\posintn!$ is
\[
\sum_{\counter =1}^{\posintn} \left\lfloor \frac{\posintn}{primep^{\counter}} \right\rfloor.
\]
(To see this, note that the $\counter$-th term counts the multiples of $primep^{\counter}$ among
$1, \dots, \posintn$, so that a number divisible exactly by $primep^{\counter}$ gets counted
exactly $\counter$ times.)  This number can be reinterpreted as the cardinality
of the set $pointset$ of points in the plane with positive integer coordinates
lying on or under the curve $ordinate = \posintn primep^{-\abscissa}$: namely, each summand is the
number of points of $pointset$ with $\abscissa=\counter$.

On the right side, the exponent of $primep$ in the prime factorization of $\lcm(1,
\dots, \lfloor \posintn/\counter \rfloor)$ is $\lfloor \log_{primep} \lfloor \posintn/\counter \rfloor \rfloor
= \lfloor \log_{primep} (\posintn/\counter) \rfloor$.  However, this is precisely the number
of points of $pointset$ with $ordinate=\counter$. Thus
\[
\sum_{\counter=1}^{\posintn}
\lfloor \log_{primep} \lfloor \posintn/\counter \rfloor \rfloor
= \sum_{\counter =1}^{\posintn} \left\lfloor \frac{\posintn}{primep^{\counter}} \right\rfloor,
\]
and the desired result follows.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We prove the result by induction on $\posintn$, the case $\posintn=1$ being obvious.
What we actually show is that going from $\posintn-1$ to $\posintn$ changes both
sides by the same multiplicative factor, that is,
\[
\posintn = \prod_{\counter=1}^{\posintn-1} \frac{\lcm\{1, 2, \dots, \lfloor \posintn/\counter \rfloor\}}{\lcm
\{1, 2, \dots, \lfloor (\posintn-1)/\counter \rfloor\}}.
\]
Note that the $\counter$-th term in the product is equal to 1 if $\posintn/\counter$ is not
an integer, i.e., if $\posintn/\counter$ is not a divisor of $\posintn$.
It is also equal to 1 if $\posintn/\counter$ is a divisor of $\posintn$ but not a prime power,
since any composite number divides the lcm of all smaller numbers.
However, if $\posintn/\counter$ is a power of $primep$, then the $\counter$-th term is equal to $primep$.

Since $\posintn/\counter$ runs over all proper divisors of $\posintn$, the product on the right
side includes one factor of the prime $primep$
for each factor of $primep$ in the prime factorization of $\posintn$. Thus the whole
product is indeed equal to $\posintn$, completing the induction.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Show that for each positive integer  rhinoceros,
\[
    rhinoceros! = \prod_{buttercup=1}^{rhinoceros}  \mathrm{lcm}\{1, 2, \dots, \lfloor rhinoceros/buttercup\rfloor\} .
\]
(Here $\mathrm{lcm}$ denotes the least common multiple, and
$\lfloor marshmallow \rfloor$ denotes the greatest integer $\leq marshmallow$.)
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
It is enough to show that for each prime chandelier, the exponent of chandelier in
the prime factorization of both sides is the same.
On the left side, it is well-known that the exponent of chandelier in the
prime factorization of rhinoceros! is
\[
\sum_{buttercup =1}^{rhinoceros} \left\lfloor \frac{rhinoceros}{chandelier^{buttercup}} \right\rfloor.
\]
(To see this, note that the buttercup-th term counts the multiples of $chandelier^{buttercup}$ among
$1, \dots, rhinoceros$, so that a number divisible exactly by $chandelier^{buttercup}$ gets counted
exactly buttercup times.)  This number can be reinterpreted as the cardinality
of the set platypus of points in the plane with positive integer coordinates
lying on or under the curve $hurricane = rhinoceros chandelier^{-marshmallow}$: namely, each summand is the
number of points of platypus with $marshmallow=buttercup$.

On the right side, the exponent of chandelier in the prime factorization of $\lcm(1,
\dots, \lfloor rhinoceros/buttercup \rfloor)$ is $\lfloor \log_{chandelier} \lfloor rhinoceros/buttercup \rfloor \rfloor
= \lfloor \log_{chandelier} (rhinoceros/buttercup) \rfloor$.  However, this is precisely the number
of points of platypus with $hurricane=buttercup$. Thus
\[
\sum_{buttercup=1}^{rhinoceros}
\lfloor \log_{chandelier} \lfloor rhinoceros/buttercup \rfloor \rfloor
= \sum_{buttercup =1}^{rhinoceros} \left\lfloor \frac{rhinoceros}{chandelier^{buttercup}} \right\rfloor,
\]
and the desired result follows.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We prove the result by induction on rhinoceros, the case $rhinoceros=1$ being obvious.
What we actually show is that going from $rhinoceros-1$ to $rhinoceros$ changes both
sides by the same multiplicative factor, that is,
\[
rhinoceros = \prod_{buttercup=1}^{rhinoceros-1} \frac{\lcm\{1, 2, \dots, \lfloor rhinoceros/buttercup \rfloor\}}{\lcm
\{1, 2, \dots, \lfloor (rhinoceros-1)/buttercup \rfloor\}}.
\]
Note that the buttercup-th term in the product is equal to 1 if $rhinoceros/buttercup$ is not
an integer, i.e., if $rhinoceros/buttercup$ is not a divisor of rhinoceros.
It is also equal to 1 if $rhinoceros/buttercup$ is a divisor of rhinoceros but not a prime power,
since any composite number divides the lcm of all smaller numbers.
However, if $rhinoceros/buttercup$ is a power of chandelier, then the buttercup-th term is equal to chandelier.

Since $rhinoceros/buttercup$ runs over all proper divisors of rhinoceros, the product on the right
side includes one factor of the prime chandelier
for each factor of chandelier in the prime factorization of rhinoceros. Thus the whole
product is indeed equal to rhinoceros, completing the induction.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Show that for each positive integer  boundless,
\[
    boundless! = \prod_{aggregate=1}^{boundless}  \mathrm{lcm}\{1, 2, \dots, \lfloor boundless/aggregate\rfloor\} .
\]
(Here $\mathrm{lcm}$ denotes the least common multiple, and
$\lfloor ordinate \rfloor$ denotes the greatest integer $\leq ordinate$.)
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
It is enough to show that for each prime $composite$, the exponent of $composite$ in
the prime factorization of both sides is the same.
On the left side, it is well-known that the exponent of $composite$ in the
prime factorization of $boundless!$ is
\[
\sum_{aggregate =1}^{boundless} \left\lfloor \frac{boundless}{composite^{aggregate}} \right\rfloor.
\]
(To see this, note that the $aggregate$-th term counts the multiples of $composite^{aggregate}$ among
$1, \dots, boundless$, so that a number divisible exactly by $composite^{aggregate}$ gets counted
exactly $aggregate$ times.)  This number can be reinterpreted as the cardinality
of the set $complement$ of points in the plane with positive integer coordinates
lying on or under the curve $abscissa = boundless composite^{-ordinate}$: namely, each summand is the
number of points of $complement$ with $ordinate=aggregate$.

On the right side, the exponent of $composite$ in the prime factorization of $\lcm(1,
\dots, \lfloor boundless/aggregate \rfloor)$ is $\lfloor \log_{composite} \lfloor boundless/aggregate \rfloor \rfloor
= \lfloor \log_{composite} (boundless/aggregate) \rfloor$.  However, this is precisely the number
of points of $complement$ with $abscissa=aggregate$. Thus
\[
\sum_{aggregate=1}^{boundless}
\lfloor \log_{composite} \lfloor boundless/aggregate \rfloor \rfloor
= \sum_{aggregate =1}^{boundless} \left\lfloor \frac{boundless}{composite^{aggregate}} \right\rfloor,
\]
and the desired result follows.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We prove the result by induction on $boundless$, the case $boundless=1$ being obvious.
What we actually show is that going from $boundless-1$ to $boundless$ changes both
sides by the same multiplicative factor, that is,
\[
boundless = \prod_{aggregate=1}^{boundless-1} \frac{\lcm\{1, 2, \dots, \lfloor boundless/aggregate \rfloor\}}{\lcm
\{1, 2, \dots, \lfloor (boundless-1)/aggregate \rfloor\}}.
\]
Note that the $aggregate$-th term in the product is equal to 1 if $boundless/aggregate$ is not
an integer, i.e., if $boundless/aggregate$ is not a divisor of $boundless$.
It is also equal to 1 if $boundless/aggregate$ is a divisor of $boundless$ but not a prime power,
since any composite number divides the lcm of all smaller numbers.
However, if $boundless/aggregate$ is a power of $composite$, then the $aggregate$-th term is equal to $composite$.

Since $boundless/aggregate$ runs over all proper divisors of $boundless$, the product on the right
side includes one factor of the prime $composite$
for each factor of $composite$ in the prime factorization of $boundless$. Thus the whole
product is indeed equal to $boundless$, completing the induction.

garbled_string.question:
Show that for each positive integer  qzxwvtnp,
\[
    qzxwvtnp! = \prod_{mdfplrqe=1}^{qzxwvtnp}  \mathrm{lcm}\{1, 2, \dots, \lfloor qzxwvtnp/mdfplrqe\rfloor\} .
\]
(Here $\mathrm{lcm}$ denotes the least common multiple, and
$\lfloor tcnvrbsa \rfloor$ denotes the greatest integer $\leq tcnvrbsa$.)
garbled_string.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
It is enough to show that for each prime hjgrksla, the exponent of hjgrksla in
the prime factorization of both sides is the same.
On the left side, it is well-known that the exponent of hjgrksla in the
prime factorization of qzxwvtnp! is
\[
\sum_{mdfplrqe =1}^{qzxwvtnp} \left\lfloor \frac{qzxwvtnp}{hjgrksla^{mdfplrqe}} \right\rfloor.
\]
(To see this, note that the mdfplrqe-th term counts the multiples of hjgrksla^{mdfplrqe} among
1, \dots, qzxwvtnp, so that a number divisible exactly by hjgrksla^{mdfplrqe} gets counted
exactly mdfplrqe times.)  This number can be reinterpreted as the cardinality
of the set wrvjhbkd of points in the plane with positive integer coordinates
lying on or under the curve gzkqfmno = qzxwvtnp hjgrksla^{-tcnvrbsa}: namely, each summand is the
number of points of wrvjhbkd with tcnvrbsa=mdfplrqe.

On the right side, the exponent of hjgrksla in the prime factorization of $\lcm(1,
\dots, \lfloor qzxwvtnp/mdfplrqe \rfloor)$ is $\lfloor \log_{hjgrksla} \lfloor qzxwvtnp/mdfplrqe \rfloor \rfloor
= \lfloor \log_{hjgrksla} (qzxwvtnp/mdfplrqe) \rfloor$.  However, this is precisely the number
of points of wrvjhbkd with gzkqfmno=mdfplrqe. Thus
\[
\sum_{mdfplrqe=1}^{qzxwvtnp}
\lfloor \log_{hjgrksla} \lfloor qzxwvtnp/mdfplrqe \rfloor \rfloor
= \sum_{mdfplrqe =1}^{qzxwvtnp} \left\lfloor \frac{qzxwvtnp}{hjgrksla^{mdfplrqe}} \right\rfloor,
\]
and the desired result follows.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We prove the result by induction on qzxwvtnp, the case qzxwvtnp=1 being obvious.
What we actually show is that going from qzxwvtnp-1 to qzxwvtnp changes both
sides by the same multiplicative factor, that is,
\[
qzxwvtnp = \prod_{mdfplrqe=1}^{qzxwvtnp-1} \frac{\lcm\{1, 2, \dots, \lfloor qzxwvtnp/mdfplrqe \rfloor\}}{\lcm
\{1, 2, \dots, \lfloor (qzxwvtnp-1)/mdfplrqe \rfloor\}}.
\]
Note that the mdfplrqe-th term in the product is equal to 1 if qzxwvtnp/mdfplrqe is not
an integer, i.e., if qzxwvtnp/mdfplrqe is not a divisor of qzxwvtnp.
It is also equal to 1 if qzxwvtnp/mdfplrqe is a divisor of qzxwvtnp but not a prime power,
since any composite number divides the lcm of all smaller numbers.
However, if qzxwvtnp/mdfplrqe is a power of hjgrksla, then the mdfplrqe-th term is equal to hjgrksla.

Since qzxwvtnp/mdfplrqe runs over all proper divisors of qzxwvtnp, the product on the right
side includes one factor of the prime hjgrksla
for each factor of hjgrksla in the prime factorization of qzxwvtnp. Thus the whole
product is indeed equal to qzxwvtnp, completing the induction.

kernel_variant.question:
Let K be a number field with ring of integers O_K.  
For a finite non-empty subset S \subset  O_K \ {0} define the ideal-theoretic least common multiple by  

  lcm_{O_K}(S) := \bigcap _{\beta \in S} \beta  O_K  
        = \bigcap _{p} p^{\,max_{\beta \in S} v_{p}(\beta )},  

and put lcm_{O_K}(\emptyset )=O_K.

Prove that for every positive integer n one has the following equality of (principal) ideals of O_K:  

           (n!) O_K = \prod _{i=1}^{n} lcm_{O_K}{1,2,\ldots ,\lfloor n/i\rfloor }.  (\star )

(Here \lfloor x\rfloor  denotes the greatest integer \leq  x.  For indices i with \lfloor n/i\rfloor =0 the empty lcm is interpreted as O_K and hence contributes the unit ideal.)
kernel_variant.solution:
We work in the Dedekind domain O_K.  An ideal is uniquely determined by its valuations at all non-zero prime ideals, so it suffices to compare the p-adic valuations on both sides of (\star ) for every prime ideal p\subset O_K.

Notation  
* p := p \cap  \mathbb{Z} is the rational prime below p;  
* e_p and f_p are the ramification index and residue degree of p over p;  
* v_p(\cdot ) is the usual p-adic valuation on \mathbb{Z}.  
Because \mathbb{Z} \to  O_K is integral, for any m \in  \mathbb{Z} we have  

  v_{p}(m)=e_{p}\cdot v_p(m).                                        (1)

Step 1. The p-adic valuation of the left-hand side  
Applying (1) together with Legendre's formula gives  

 v_{p}(n!)=e_{p}\cdot v_p(n!)=e_{p}\cdot \sum _{k\geq 1}\lfloor n/p^{k}\rfloor .             (2)

Step 2. The p-adic valuation of each factor on the right  
Fix i with 1 \leq  i \leq  n and set m_i:=\lfloor n/i\rfloor .  In the list 1,2,\ldots ,m_i the largest power of p dividing any entry is p^{\lfloor log_p m_i\rfloor }; hence  

 v_p(lcm_{\mathbb{Z}}{1,\ldots ,m_i}) = \lfloor log_p m_i\rfloor .                         (3)

Using (1) we get  

 v_{p}(lcm_{O_K}{1,\ldots ,m_i}) = e_{p}\cdot \lfloor log_p m_i\rfloor .             (4)

Step 3. The p-adic valuation of the complete product  
Summing (4) over i gives  

 v_{p} (RHS of (\star )) = \sum _{i=1}^{n} e_{p}\cdot \lfloor log_p \lfloor n/i\rfloor \rfloor   
           = e_{p}\cdot \sum _{i=1}^{n}\lfloor log_p \lfloor n/i\rfloor \rfloor .             (5)

Step 4. A combinatorial identity  
Exactly as in the classical integer proof, one shows  

  \sum _{i=1}^{n}\lfloor log_p \lfloor n/i\rfloor \rfloor  = \sum _{k\geq 1}\lfloor n/p^{k}\rfloor .                (6)

(A convenient interpretation is that both sides count the lattice points with positive integral coordinates lying on or beneath the curve y = n p^{-x}.)

Substituting (6) into (5) yields  

 v_{p}(RHS) = e_{p}\cdot \sum _{k\geq 1}\lfloor n/p^{k}\rfloor .                        (7)

Step 5. Equality of valuations  
Comparing (2) and (7) we have v_{p}(LHS)=v_{p}(RHS) for every p, hence the ideals agree:

  (n!) O_K = \prod _{i=1}^{n} lcm_{O_K}{1,2,\ldots ,\lfloor n/i\rfloor }. \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• The original statement concerns ordinary integers; the enhanced variant moves to the ring of integers of an arbitrary number field, forcing the solver to work simultaneously with all prime ideals lying over each rational prime.  
• One must understand ideal–theoretic lcms, ramification indices, and how valuations behave on principal ideals generated by rational integers (formula (1)).  
• The Legendre formula has to be lifted to every 𝔭, introducing the ramification factor e_𝔭.  
• The combinatorial lattice-point argument survives but now has to be carefully interwoven with algebraic-number-theoretic data.  
• All of these ingredients—Dedekind domains, prime ideal decomposition, ramification, and valuation theory—are far beyond the elementary techniques needed for the original identity, making this kernel variant substantially more intricate and conceptually deeper.
